utterances: Susan Morrison, editor of the new book, ""Thirty Ways of Looking at Hillary"" and one of her contributors, Dahlia Lithwick.
You're listening to TALK OF THE NATION from NPR News.
And Elizabeth(ph) is on the line.
Elizabeth calling from Jacksonville, Florida.
Yes.
I think my feeling, like so many others, are ambivalence.
Because on one hand, she does come across as this very strong female that you admire, you're in sympathy with her, especially with all the excoriations(ph) she has taken over the past 16 years.
And then there are other times she comes out as this, you know, very vulnerable person, vulnerable female and that's okay.
I think part of our problem is trying to put everybody into these neat little boxes.

And I want to comment on the statement that was just made, while African-American men may have had the vote 50 years before women, let us not even try to fool ourselves and think that African-American males especially, males especially, have had it any easier than white women.
That is just not so in corporate America.
You know, the white female has always been given the benefit of the doubt over the black males.
So I think we all need to just step back and, you know, let the facts play out to themselves and look at the issues and then maybe try not to vote just strictly on personality.
But as human beings we all do that.
I know that.
We earlier played that famous clip of Senator Clinton in New Hampshire having that emotional moment.
But lately, she's also been showing her fangs a bit.
Here's an attack ad that's been running in South Carolina.
(Soundbite of political ad)
Listen to Barack Obama last week talking about Republicans.
It turns me off.
It turns - I voted for Bill Clinton.
I've always - if he had to run again, yes, I'll vote for him.
And I guess he is running again and I will not vote for him because I think it is going overboard.
I think every news outlet have done fact check in terms of the statement and other statements, and they are distorting.
And the last thing we want, the last thing we want is to sort of replicate the Karl Rove style.
And I just think this is turning off too many people.
Dahlia Lithwick, let me ask you, how would that kind of performance figure to your analysis?
I think that this goes back to something Susan said at the very beginning that's so important, the sort of Deborah Tannen point about language.
I think not only is there a sort of very, very, very crabbed universe in which women can operate without getting called shrill and, you know, strident and all those words.
As Susan said, that are not (unintelligible) about men.

I think it goes the other way, which is a very narrow bandwidth in which they can sort of speak politically without looking like they've ran afoul some, you know, invisible goal that we don't know where it is.

And so I think that whereas men can talk to each other the way Obama and Clinton talked to each other on the Monday debate without raising all these questions of, you know, is this too much, is it over the line, women still can't do that.
Elizabeth, thanks so much for the call.
Appreciate it.
Thank you.
And we're going to continue our conversation on the other side of the break.
So stay with us.
We're talking with Susan Morrison, the editor of the new book ""Thirty Ways of Looking at Hillary."" Also with us, Dahlia Lithwick, one of her contributors.
Robin Givhan, who also contributed to the essays, will join us when we return.
800-989-8255, if you'd like to join us.
I'm Neal Conan.
It's the TALK OF THE NATION from NPR News.
(Soundbite of music)
This is TALK OF THE NATION.
I'm Neal Conan in Washington.
Right now, were talking about women and Hillary Rodham Clinton.
If you'd like to read the introduction to the book, ""Thirty Ways of Looking at Hillary,"" you can find it at our Web site, npr.org/talk.
Our guests are Dahlia Lithwick, who's one of the contributors to the book.
And Susan Morrison, editor of the book, ""Thirty Ways of Looking at Hillary.
Style has one more issue that follows Hillary Clinton around her during her campaign, from her infamous headband in the early days to cleavage-gate.
Joining us now is Robin Givhan, fashion editor for The Washington Post and winner of the 2006 Pulitzer Prize for criticism.
Robin is also with us from our bureau in New York.
Nice to speak with you again.
Nice to be here.

And you write in your essay, modern women like to pretend that style doesn't matter anymore, that feminism and modernity have shattered the old notion that there was a value in a woman being able to look effortlessly chic.
Why does style matter?
Well, I think style matters because we're clearly a very visual culture and we often only have moments to make - to form an opinion about someone.
And I think style is a way - is a shorthand - it's a way that we have of summing someone up, figuring out what tribe they belong to, figuring out how they perhaps think of themselves.
And it's - I think it's also a way of making connections with people.
It's also a lightning rod.

When you reported on the fact that Senator Clinton was wearing an outfit that showed a little bit of cleavage - as compared with her normal black pantsuit, that provoked quite a response not just from Senator Clinton's campaign but from a lot of readers, to The Washington Post as well.
Indeed, it did.
(Soundbite of laughter)

And, you know, I was surprised by that reaction, quite honestly, in part because I thought that in many ways, much of what I was saying was something that most people noticed and perhaps were commenting on or thinking about themselves.
And the fact was that she was wearing something that was very much out of character for her.
I mean, she's known as someone who is very buttoned up.

You know, I thought about back to her days as first lady and the choices that she made when she was essentially in the position of being this representative of American femininity - for a lack of a better way of describing the first lady's ceremonial role.
And here was something that was very surprising for the person who is wearing it as well as for the location.
And I think it said something about her comfort level with her personal presentation.
And the reaction, I think, clearly said something about the way that we view women.
Well, let's get some callers in on the conversation again.
Again, the number is 800-989-8255.
You can also send us e-mail, talk@npr.org.
Let's turn next to Dexter(ph).
Dexter with us from Dothan, Alabama.
Hi.

I was wondering if any of the women had commented or mentioned the comparison between Hillary Clinton, whom I have a lot of respect for, as I also did for Jacqueline Onassis, and how Mrs. Onassis couldn't take a breath without everyone adoring the breath.
Hillary seems to be able to utter a syllable and a huge percentage of people are driven in the opposite direction.
It's just a very interesting contrast in style in a kind of overall sense.
You write in your piece, Robin Givhan, that particularly baby boomer women admire not just Hillary Clinton but Katharine Hepburn - not Katharine Hepburn, Audrey Hepburn…
Audrey.
…and Jacqueline Kennedy, as our caller Dexter suggests.
J.P.: Hear hear.
There you go.
There it is.
Those people that make it this far are those kind of people.
And I think with Jane Kramer in her essays, Susan Morrison, who talked about this thing of holding women to higher standards.
Yeah.

One of the things I do think is sort of fascinating is that as much as modern or contemporary women try to pooh-pooh the notion that fashion or style, more specifically, is any way important, when they look back on women like that, I think they do help - part of what helps to form their opinion is the style that those women projected.

And in many ways, it wasn't even so much about a particular garment or wearing a particular designer or being trendy, it was simply the way that they presented themselves which seem to speak of confidence and a kind of authenticity that I think they saw in their attire.
I think they really believed that what they were seeing in those clothes and what they were seeing in their style was a reflection of who they were as a person.
And you write - and thank you Dexter for the phone call.
You write that Hillary Clinton still hasn't become what we crave, even need, an icon of female power.
We want her to tell us, define for us, what female power looks like at the highest level.
Yeah, I think in a really basic way when we look at her, we are trying to envision her in the role of commander-in-chief.
And there's really not - there's not a template for that.
We don't really know what a woman - an American woman looks like in that role.
And so I do think that part of what we assort of struggle with is just kind of filling in the blanks, you know, trying to actually get that image in our head.
And part of that involves, well, what is that person wearing?

You know what a man looks like in that role, but - and the other thing, too, I think, is that in many ways, Hillary has played out in a very public way the struggles that a lot of women have in dealing with the fashion industry, in dealing with her own personal style, and kind of negotiating this tension between actually secretly enjoying fashion and somehow feeling that admitting to it is going to lessen their intelligence or lessen the authority that they have.
Let's get another caller on the line.
This is Irma(ph).
Irma with us from Chattanooga, Tennessee.
Hi.
Hi.
Yes.

I was calling because I just wanted to give my opinion about - I appreciate everything that everyone has said and it helps me understand a little better, but I think I'm driven to vote for Hillary or to admire her because of her strength.
Because of her strength?
Yes, she's a very strong individual.
And you know, in our woman's world, it's so very difficult, you know, it's an uphill battle everywhere - at work, anywhere you are.
And she didn't even shed a tear that time, but, you know, it sure was nice to see that she has emotions.
But some of us are just plain, oh, we cry every time, because it's so difficult for us.
And a lot of people don't understand that that we're out there working, raising a family, doing it all, and we also have to be - we have to be strong.
And I believe that that's her strength.
That's what I admire in her.
Dahlia Lithwick, I wanted to ask you about that.
You talked about the dichotomy.
Yes, you want her to be strong, but yes, you want her feminine side, too.
Right.
It's almost impossible to, sort of, negotiate both of those things at the same time.
I, sort of, somewhere in my essay say, you know, we want her to be baking cookies and, you know, commander-in-chief at the same time, and nothing less will satisfy us.
And I do think, you know, it is interesting just going back to what Robin is saying about fashion.
I think it's worth at least throwing out there.

The - one of the big contrast I would see between someone like Jackie Kennedy, who is sort of a fashion icon, and Hillary - and I think Robin makes this point in her essay - is that Hillary keeps changing, and that there is this sense that there would be four or five distinct, sort of, periods.
And I think that that really starts to smoke out an anxiety that we have of, why is this woman really reinventing herself every little while?
And why is that - or in which ways does that mirror the way she's doing so politically?
So I think there's a weird fondness for Jackie Kennedy because you have a sense that even if she was out milking cows or re-grouting the shower, she would have one of those cute little hats on.
And that makes you feel like here is a person who is comfortable in her own skin.
Hillary is much more, I think, elusive.
And I think that Robin's right that that's because she's, kind of, trying to figure it out as she goes along.
But I think it does make us feel a little bit nervous about who the there is in there?
